description: Operational psychoacoustic rules used by the mixing/mastering engine and the prompt builder. Each rule is grounded in the citations listed; speculative rules are flagged.

critical_bands_bark.doc: Bark scale critical bands. Two sounds in the same band mask each other; two in different bands coexist cleanly. Use this to schedule frequency content across a mix.

bands.label: bass-high
bands.use: low male vocal, bass overtones, guitar lows

bands.label: mid
bands.use: mud zone — cut here on guitars/keys

bands.label: mid
bands.use: vocal honk if present — usually cut

fletcher_munson.doc: Equal-loudness contour. Ear is most sensitive at 2-5 kHz. Below 60 Hz and above 10 kHz, sensitivity drops sharply, especially at low listening levels. Mixes for streaming should account for low-volume listening (phone speakers, AirPods, car).
fletcher_munson.implication: Mix at multiple volumes. A bass-heavy mix that sounds great at high volume can feel weak at low volume because the ear's bass sensitivity drops. Master should be tested at -30 dB SPL (phone speaker simulation) AND at -65 dB SPL (car) AND at -85 dB SPL (loud studio).

masking_rules.doc: Operational rules for avoiding frequency masking when arranging and mixing.

rules.rule: Kick fundamental and bass fundamental cannot be at the same frequency. Either: (a) kick lives at 60 Hz and bass at 80-100 Hz, or (b) sidechain bass to kick so they alternate in time.
rules.default: Kick at 60 Hz, bass guitar at 100 Hz with subtle high-pass at 50 Hz on bass.

rules.rule: Lead vocal lives in 1-3 kHz. Distorted electric guitar lives in 200-800 Hz with overtones up to 5 kHz. Cut guitar in 1-3 kHz to free vocal presence (the 'guitar scoop').
rules.default: Cut guitar 2-3 dB at 2.5 kHz when vocal is present.

rules.rule: Snare crack at 2-3 kHz competes with vocal sibilance. Solution: tame snare 3 kHz on solo, brighten 5 kHz on snare to give it identity above the vocal.
rules.default: Snare: cut 1 dB at 2.5 kHz, boost 1 dB at 5 kHz.

rules.rule: Hi-hats and crash cymbals both live above 5 kHz. Solution: hi-hats narrow band (6-9 kHz), cymbals broader (4-12 kHz) but level kept under hats.
rules.default: Hi-hat solo presence 7-9 kHz; cymbals tail 10-15 kHz.

rules.rule: Frequencies below ~120 Hz should be mono. Stereo bass causes phase cancellation on club systems and mono playback (phone speakers, restaurants).
rules.default: Mono-summed below 120 Hz on master bus.

perceptual_loudness.doc: LUFS (Loudness Units Full Scale) — modern loudness standard. -23 LUFS is broadcast (EBU R128). -14 LUFS is Spotify integrated. -16 LUFS is Apple Music. Mastering above the platform's normalization threshold gets turned down — there's no benefit and you sacrifice dynamic range.

roughness_and_dissonance.doc: Plomp & Levelt (1965) showed that roughness — the unpleasant beating sensation between two close-frequency tones — peaks when interval is ~1/4 of the critical bandwidth. Used to predict perceived dissonance.
guidelines: Avoid two strong tonal sources within 1/4 critical band of each other (e.g., two unison synths slightly detuned by 5-15 cents = lush; detuned by 50-80 cents = roughness).
Sevenths and ninths get their flavor from controlled roughness; minor seconds (b9) maximize it.
For meditation/healing music, avoid intervals smaller than a major third in low registers — they create unpleasant roughness.

transient_perception.doc: The first ~50 ms of a sound contains most of the perceptual identity. Transient design and attack envelopes matter more than spectral content.
implications: Punchy kick = sharp attack envelope (5-15 ms attack, fast decay)
808 = slow attack (50-80 ms) for sub feel, fast attack for percussive feel
Snare = transient at 0-10 ms with bright 2-5 kHz content
Pad = slow attack (200ms+) for ambient feel

haas_effect_doppler.doc: The Haas (precedence) effect: identical sounds within ~30 ms of each other are perceived as one source coming from the location of the FIRST sound. Used for stereo widening without phase issues.
haas_effect_doppler.implementation: Duplicate a sound, pan it ~70% to one side, delay 5-30 ms, lower its level by 2-3 dB. The brain perceives a wider source without phase cancellation.

stereo_field_rules.doc: Where to pan things and how wide.

rules.pan: double-tracked, hard L and hard R

dynamic_range_perception.doc: Compression beyond a certain point reduces emotional impact. Loud-everywhere music feels relentless and the brain disengages.
guidelines: Genre-typical dynamic range LU (loudness range): pop 4-6, rock 6-8, jazz 12-16, classical 18-24, ambient 12-20
Crest factor (peak/RMS): pop 6-8 dB, rock 8-12 dB, classical 18-24 dB
If dynamic range < 4 LU: track sounds 'crushed' — loudness war territory
The chorus should be measurably louder than the verse (1-3 LU minimum) for the brain to register as a chorus

speculative_flagged.doc: Rules we use because they work in practice but aren't peer-reviewed. Treat as heuristics.

rules.claim: Warm colors (red/orange/gold) → high arousal/aggression; cool colors (blue/purple) → low arousal/calm; green → balanced.
rules.evidence: Cultural convention, partially supported by cross-cultural emotion-color studies.
rules.use: Default video color palette by mood — overridable.

rules.claim: Lower-pitched sounds feel heavier/darker; higher feel lighter/brighter.
rules.evidence: Robust cross-modal correspondence in literature, but the magnitude varies.
rules.use: Mix decisions about which element should sit higher in pitch for emotional weight.

rules.claim: Fast tempo → red/orange visualizers; slow tempo → blue/purple/green.
rules.evidence: Convention from VJ culture, not science.
